summary:
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (1756–1791 AD) was the supreme prodigy and central figure of the Classical period in music — an Austrian composer who began performing before European courts at age six, composed his first symphony at eight, and produced 626 catalogued works by his death at 35, including 41 symphonies, 27 piano concertos, 21 operas, and extensive chamber, sacred, and solo works. Mozart's music achieved a balance of formal clarity, emotional expressiveness, and melodic invention that has made it universally accessible across two centuries — his operas (The Magic Flute, Don Giovanni, The Marriage of Figaro, Così fan tutte) defined the operatic canon, his piano concertos established the form's standard, and his final Jupiter Symphony demonstrated the highest achievement of classical symphonic structure.

Mozart's life was shaped by the tension between his extraordinary gifts and his limited success in securing the stable aristocratic employment that musicians required in the 18th century. His father Leopold devoted his childhood to displaying him as a prodigy across Europe, creating the celebrity and musical education that formed his genius — but also stunting his personal development and leaving him dependent on patronage structures he struggled to navigate. His break with the Archbishop of Salzburg (1781) and move to Vienna as a freelance musician was a bold gamble on a new economic model for composers — concerts, publications, and private pupils rather than court salary — that partially succeeded but left him chronically short of money. His death at 35 (from disputed causes) was mourned by those who recognized his genius, and his unfinished Requiem has become one of music history's most poignant symbols of unfulfilled potential. Mozart and his near-contemporary Haydn defined the Classical style; Mozart's student Beethoven would transform it into Romanticism.
